weather: Today in Edmonds, Washington: Sunny and pleasant. High 59-64°F, low 45-48°F. Light north winds 5-10 mph. Minimal rain chance (0-10%). Clear evening turning partly cloudy overnight.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council approved key contracts and budget amendments during its April 7 business meeting. The council unanimously approved a $415,116 contract with Jacobs Engineering to support commissioning of the gasification system at the wastewater treatment plant. They also approved a $419,000 contract for sewer pipe rehabilitation at 27 locations identified in a 2013 review. The council declined to pursue a counterpetition in the Redelfs tree litigation matter, where a 33-inch-diameter landmark tree was removed without a permit, resulting in a reduced fine from $102,245 to $20,000. Additionally, the council declared April 9 as Local News Day to recognize the value of local journalism.

After three years of development and setbacks, Edmonds is moving forward with operating its innovative gasification system at the wastewater treatment plant. The city approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to assist with operations through 2027. The system, which converts human waste into dried solids and destroys PFAS "forever chemicals," was the first of its kind in the country. The project has faced numerous technical challenges since initial attempts in 2023, including equipment failures and operational issues.

Edmonds will begin a major sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation project on May 4, targeting 27 locations with failing infrastructure identified in a 2013 review. The 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project will use trenchless technology to repair aging sewer and storm pipes throughout the city. This project is part of ongoing infrastructure improvements to address aging utility systems.

The South County Fire Board of Commissioners is working on redistricting proposals to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority's five commissioner districts. Two plans have been presented: Plan 1 would place almost all of Edmonds into a single district, while Plan 2 would split the city along Pine Street between two districts. The board is seeking public input before finalizing the redistricting plan, which will determine how Edmonds residents are represented on the fire commission.

The City of Edmonds hosted an open house to share the 60% design plan for Stage 3 of the Highway 99 Revitalization Project. This represents the next phase of the multi-year effort to improve the Highway 99 corridor through Edmonds, with public input being sought on the current design proposals.

global_news.findings: President Trump stated at a White House state dinner for King Charles that the British monarch "does not want Iran to have a nuclear weapon," introducing the Middle East conflict into royal proceedings. The comments came during King Charles' historic four-day US state visit, the first since Queen Elizabeth II in 2007. Trump claimed "we're never going to let that opponent have a nuclear weapon," though King Charles made no direct reference to Iran in his own remarks.

King Charles delivered a historic address to the US Congress, calling for "reconciliation and renewal" between the US and UK at a tense moment in transatlantic relations. The King emphasized that "time and again, our two countries have always found ways to come together" and stressed the importance of defending democratic values through continued support for NATO and Ukraine. The speech came amid President Trump's criticism of British Prime Minister Keir Starmer over the UK's response to the Iran conflict.

The United Arab Emirates announced its shock exit from OPEC, dealing a significant blow to the 65-year-old oil cartel that produces 40% of the world's crude oil. The UAE's departure comes during an unprecedented energy crisis caused by the Iran war and the ongoing blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. The move has shaken global oil markets and raised questions about OPEC's future influence over energy prices.

A Russian superyacht linked to sanctioned billionaire Alexei Mordashov, valued at over $500 million, successfully sailed through the Strait of Hormuz despite the ongoing blockade. The 142-meter vessel 'Nord' traveled from Dubai to Oman over the weekend, representing one of the few private vessels to transit the critical waterway. The passage highlights the complex dynamics of sanctions enforcement during the Iran conflict, as approximately 20% of global oil supplies normally pass through the strait.

A devastating train collision near Jakarta, Indonesia killed at least 15 people and injured 88 others when a long-distance train crashed into a stationary commuter train in Bekasi. The accident occurred in the female-only carriage of the commuter train, with rescue efforts ongoing. Indonesian President Joko Widodo has ordered a comprehensive investigation into the crash, which has prompted national mourning and raised concerns about railway safety standards.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping ProPublica investigation reveals that the Trump administration has systematically dismantled federal guardrails that once protected U.S. elections, replacing career staff with figures from the election-denial movement. At least 75 specialists have resigned or been reassigned, while two dozen new appointees—ten of whom previously tried to overturn the 2020 vote—now wield unprecedented authority. The report reconstructs a December 2020 meeting in which Attorney General William Barr asked CISA and FBI experts whether voting machines had been hacked; when told the Antrim County, Michiga [TRUNCATED]

In a second major feature, ProPublica and FRONTLINE document more than 300 arrests of U.S. citizens—protesters, bystanders, even family members—during Trump-era immigration sweeps. Federal agents routinely charged dissenters with assault or conspiracy under statutes designed for organized crime, but over one-third of the cases collapsed once video evidence contradicted officers’ statements. Prosecutors quietly dismissed charges or lost at trial, yet the arrests achieved their intended chilling effect: activists describe abandoning demonstrations for fear of indefinite detention. The pattern, r [TRUNCATED]

Denva Gallant’s long essay in Aeon uses the 13th-century mosaics of Saint Mark’s Basilica to show how medieval art turned the human body into a political technology. In the Zen Chapel vault, Saint Mark is dragged by chains yet remains impassive; his tormentors include a conspicuously Black executioner whose stereotyped features encode racial hierarchy. Gallant argues that such images did not merely illustrate suffering—they trained viewers to read composure as sanctity and violence as legitimate when performed by certain bodies. The essay traces how repeated visual tropes naturalized power rel [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy revisits Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay “On Reading” to defend literature as a uniquely disinterested form of freedom. Against John Ruskin’s moral utilitarianism, Proust argued that books do not make us “better” in any measurable sense; instead, they preserve fugitive sensations and resurrect lost time, allowing readers to inhabit consciousnesses beyond their own. Champy extends the claim to the present, suggesting that in an era when truth is routinely instrumentalized, the slow, solitary practice of reading offers a rare space where meaning is pursued without immediate payoff—an imp [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova’s lyrical meditation on suffering reframes heartbreak not as damage to be repaired but as evidence of life’s irrepressible motion. Drawing on Ursula K. Le Guin’s The Dispossessed, she cites the physicist Shevek’s realization that “suffering is the condition on which we live,” a misunderstanding only insofar as we treat it as an aberration rather than the medium through which the self dissolves and reforms. Popova’s poem “Corrective for a Broken Heart” insists there are no pieces to pick up—only the continuous drift of a magnetic north that itself keeps moving.

A second Marginalian essay exhumes the forgotten 19th-century radical Fanny Wright, whose Nashoba commune in Tennessee attempted interracial utopia and free love decades before such experiments became fashionable. Popova shows how Wright’s scientific materialism—she translated Epicurean atomism for American readers—underpinned her abolitionism and feminism, arguing that bodily autonomy and intellectual freedom were inseparable. Walt Whitman, who devoured Wright’s Free Enquirer as a boy, later recalled her as “a brilliant woman… whose orbit was a great deal larger” than her era could tolerate.

wearable_ai.findings: The AI wearable landscape has exploded with voice-first pendants and pins. **Friend** (friend.com) leads with $7.9M in funding, offering a $99 AI companion pendant that listens and responds via voice. **Limitless** (limitless.ai) provides a $399 pendant bundle with HIPAA-grade privacy, recording conversations for personalized AI insights. **Omi** (omi.me) stands out with 3,000+ open-source integra [TRUNCATED]

**MagicSchool** remains the dominant platform for English and History teachers, offering free core tools that save educators 7+ hours weekly. New April 2026 updates include an Educational Song Generator and Studio Mode in Raina for content refinement. **Diffit** provides differentiated reading materials with free tier access for generating leveled texts, questions, and vocabulary from any topic. T [TRUNCATED]

The lifelogging pendant market has matured beyond simple recording to comprehensive life-capture systems. **Nuna** (nunatechnology.com) offers emotion-tracking pendants with meditation modes and daily mood reports. **Limitless** and **Friend** dominate with conversation-recording pendants that build searchable memory libraries. **Omi** provides the most open ecosystem with 3,000+ integrations, whi [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three 2025-2026 works advance triadic-relational metaphysics: Migliorini fuses Trinitarian theology with quantum-friendly relational ontology to escape substance monism; Isabelle proposes Triadic Relational Ontology (TRO) where a third “relational field” irreducibly mediates every dyad, making meaning contingent on sustained tension; and a new Karl Alber volume collects interdisciplinary essays charting a “Trinitarian transformation of philosophy,” bridging analytic and continental approaches to triadic being.

ai_systems.findings: Three new 2026 papers mark the maturation of autonomous research systems. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) introduces a unified three-subsystem architecture—generation, verification, evolution—capable of end-to-end scientific discovery that coordinates both computational modeling and wet-lab experimentation, achieving state-of-the-art on GAIA, HLE, GPQA and FrontierScience benchmarks. Mimosa (arXiv:2603.28986) moves beyond fixed workflows: its meta-orchestrator dynamically synthesizes task-specific multi-agent topologies, uses the Model Context Protocol for live tool discovery, and iteratively refines pipelines via an LLM judge, reaching 43.1 % success on ScienceAgentBench. MARS (arXiv:2602.02660) tackles cost-aware AI research automation with budget-constrained Monte-Carlo tree search, modular “Design-Decompose-Implement” pipelines, and reflective memory that transfers insights across branches—63 % of its lessons come from cross-branch transfer, setting a new open-source bar on MLE- [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP disclosure efforts intensified this week as Rep. Anna Paulina Luna's Task Force demanded 46 classified military videos by April 14—a deadline the Pentagon missed. Rep. Jared Moskowitz now calls for hearings on both the stalled release and the disturbing pattern of missing scientists connected to UAP programs, including retired Air Force General Neil McCasland, who vanished February 27 after Trump's disclosure order. A classified March 25 SCIF briefing, described by Rep. Eric Burlison as "one of the most bipartisan topics in Washington," reportedly covered McCasland's disappearance and advanced aerospace threats. The House Oversight Committee maintains AARO has been "less than adequate" in responses, while Defense Secretary Hegseth claims "full compliance" with Trump's February directive to release UAP files. The standoff escalates as Congress weighs subpoena power against Pentagon claims of national security constraints.

newyorker.title: The Talk of the Town: April 27, 2026
newyorker.dek: Notes from the New Yorker’s editorial notebook
newyorker.text:
The week began with a small earthquake in Manhattan, a 2.8-magnitude tremor that rattled coffee cups on the Upper West Side and prompted at least one novelist to tweet that the city was “finally expressing its feelings.” Seismologists at Columbia University assured residents that the quake was shallow and harmless, the geological equivalent of a shrug. Still, some New Yorkers reported feeling “existentially unsettled,” as if the city had briefly acknowledged their presence.

At the Met, the Costume Institute’s new exhibition, “Threaded Selves: Fashion and Identity in the Age of AI,” opened to a crowd that included both human celebrities and their digital avatars. One attendee arrived as a hologram of herself wearing a dress that changed color based on the emotional tone of nearby tweets. Another guest, the actress Tessa Thompson, wore a vintage Schiaparelli gown that had been retrofitted with fiber-optic threads to display real-time headlines from the New York Times. The exhibition’s curator, Andrew Bolton, described the show as “a meditation on how clothing now mediates not just our bodies but our data.”

In Brooklyn, a new restaurant called “Second Nature” has begun serving meals prepared entirely by AI sous-chefs. The menu, which changes daily based on weather patterns, stock market fluctuations, and the chef-bot’s reading of the Financial Times, includes dishes like “Bear Market Risotto” and “Cloudy Day Carbonara.” Diners are invited to rate each plate on a scale of “algorithmic satisfaction,” which the kitchen uses to refine its models. One patron noted that the pasta was “uncannily comforting, like being hugged by a neural net.”

Meanwhile, the New Yorker’s own offices have become a testing ground for a new AI tool designed to generate “Talk of the Town” items. The system, nicknamed “Townie,” was fed every Talk piece since 1925 and instructed to produce a paragraph in the style of E. B. White. The result: “A raccoon was seen on the fire escape of a West Village brownstone, apparently reading a copy of The New Yorker. When approached, it fled, leaving behind a half-eaten bagel and a note that read, ‘More Janet Malcolm, please.’” The staff is divided on whether to publish it.

Finally, in Central Park, a group of birdwatchers reported seeing a red-tailed hawk carrying what appeared to be a small drone in its talons. The hawk, which has been tagged by the Urban Rangers as “Theresa,” was later observed attempting to feed the drone to its chicks. Ornithologists speculate that the bird mistook the device for prey, though some conspiracy-minded observers wondered if the hawk had been trained by the Parks Department for aerial surveillance. The drone, a DJI Mini 4, was recovered intact, with a note taped to its side: “Not food. Please return to owner.”

enriched_articles.title: The UAE's exit from OPEC shakes up the oil alliance
enriched_articles.text: The UAE's decision to leave OPEC has sent shockwaves through the global oil alliance, weakening the cartel's influence over oil prices worldwide. The departure, set to take effect Friday, comes as oil prices remain elevated at $110.74 per barrel for Brent crude and $99.13 for WTI crude. The move represents the largest defection in OPEC's history and could signal further departures from the alliance that controls roughly 40% of global oil supply.

enriched_articles.title: Medieval art makes the body a site of theology and politics
enriched_articles.text: Medieval artists depicted bodies as vehicles for politics and hierarchy, with repeated imagery making these roles appear natural. The essay examines 13th-century mosaics at Saint Mark's Basilica in Venice, showing how Saint Mark's composed suffering and the Black executioner's violence create a visual system that distributes roles across bodies. These representations established patterns where certain bodies became channels of grace while others were assigned violence, influencing how societies

enriched_articles.title: The role of literature as the key to personal freedom
enriched_articles.text: Flora Champy's essay explores how literature serves as a key to personal freedom rather than moral betterment. Drawing on Marcel Proust's disagreement with John Ruskin's utilitarian view of reading, the piece argues that books create unique forms of communication that allow readers to connect with their deepest selves and access immortality through contact with the past. Literature's value lies not in moral instruction but in expanding experience beyond measure.

enriched_articles.title: Grace Against Gravity and the Physics of Vulnerability: How Birds Fly and Why They Flock in a V Formation
enriched_articles.text: Maria Popova's meditation on avian flight explores the physics and poetry of how birds achieve the impossible - defying gravity through active surrender to natural laws. The essay examines the aerodynamic principles behind V-formation flight, where birds conserve 20-30% energy by drafting in each other's wake, creating a metaphor for community vulnerability and trust where individuals take turns shielding others from life's pressures.

enriched_articles.title: Trump says King Charles agrees Iran should not have nuclear weapons
enriched_articles.text: President Trump claimed that King Charles III agrees with him that Iran should not possess nuclear weapons, making the statement during the King's historic state visit to Washington. The comment came amid ongoing tensions over Iran's nuclear program and the recent US-Iran clash at the United Nations. The King's four-day visit marks the first time a British monarch has addressed the US Congress since 1985.
